title: Kabuli Pulao

ingredients.name: basmati rice, soaked for 30 minutes and drained
visual_checks: Soaking is important — it reduces cooking time and helps the grains stay long and separate.
ingredients.notes: Soaking is important — it reduces cooking time and helps the grains stay long and separate.

ingredients.name: bone-in lamb or mutton pieces
visual_checks: Shoulder pieces give the most flavourful meat and broth.
ingredients.notes: Shoulder pieces give the most flavourful meat and broth.

subs.name: 600 g bone-in chicken pieces — reduce the braising time to 25 minutes in step 2.

ingredients.name: carrot, peeled and cut into thin matchsticks
visual_checks: Julienne to about 4 cm length; they soften and caramelise into sweet amber ribbons.
ingredients.notes: Julienne to about 4 cm length; they soften and caramelise into sweet amber ribbons.

ingredients.name: raisins
visual_checks: Rinse and soak in warm water for 10 minutes to plump them before using.
ingredients.notes: Rinse and soak in warm water for 10 minutes to plump them before using.

subs.name: Barberries (zereshk) if available at Persian grocery stores — their sharp flavour is more traditional than raisins in some Afghan households.

ingredients.name: blanched almonds or almond flakes
visual_checks: Toasted until golden before scattering over the finished pulao.
ingredients.notes: Toasted until golden before scattering over the finished pulao.

subs.name: Toasted cashew halves or pistachio slivers work equally well as a garnish; pistachios give a striking green colour.

ingredients.name: sugar
visual_checks: Used to caramelise the carrots and raisins; a small amount deepens their sweetness.
ingredients.notes: Used to caramelise the carrots and raisins; a small amount deepens their sweetness.

ingredients.name: cooking oil
visual_checks: Use a neutral oil; ghee can replace 2 tbsp of oil for extra richness.
ingredients.notes: Use a neutral oil; ghee can replace 2 tbsp of oil for extra richness.

steps.text: Heat 3 tbsp oil in a large, heavy-bottomed pot over high heat. Add the mutton pieces in a single layer and brown on all sides for 8–10 minutes without moving them too early. Remove and set aside. In the same oil, fry the sliced onion until deeply golden — about 12 minutes.

Return the browned mutton to the pot with the fried onion. Add cardamom, cinnamon stick, cloves, black pepper, cumin, and 1 tsp salt. Pour in 800 ml water, bring to a boil, then cover and simmer on low heat for 45 minutes until the lamb is tender. Taste the broth — it should be flavourful and well-seasoned, as it will cook the rice. Remove the lamb with a slotted spoon; strain and reserve the broth (you need about 600 ml).

In a separate frying pan, heat 2 tbsp oil over medium heat. Add the carrot matchsticks and stir-fry for 3 minutes. Add the drained, soaked raisins and the sugar. Cook, stirring, for 5 more minutes until the carrots are tender and caramelised to a golden-orange, and the raisins have plumped and glistened. Set aside. In the same pan, toast the almonds (if using) until golden — about 2 minutes.

Return the reserved broth to the large pot and bring to a boil. Add the drained soaked rice and 1 tsp salt. Boil uncovered on high heat, stirring once, until the rice has absorbed most of the broth and small craters appear on the surface — about 8 minutes. The rice should be about 80% cooked at this stage.

Arrange the braised lamb pieces on top of the half-cooked rice. Spread the caramelised carrots and raisins over the lamb and rice. Cover the pot tightly with a lid sealed with a damp cloth or foil to trap all steam. Cook on the lowest possible flame for 25 minutes — this is the dum stage, where the steam perfumes the whole pot and finishes the rice.

Turn off the heat and let the pot rest, still covered, for 10 minutes. Open, scatter the toasted almonds, and gently lift the lamb and carrot layers off the rice with a wide spatula — do not mix. Serve the rice first on a large platter, with the lamb pieces arranged on top and the carrot-raisin-almond garnish mounded over everything.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Use an appropriate food thermometer and safe doneness guidance for the specific cut of meat.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: mutton → 600 g bone-in chicken pieces — reduce the braising time to 25 minutes in step 2.; almond → Toasted cashew halves or pistachio slivers work equally well as a garnish; pistachios give a striking green colour.; raisin → Barberries (zereshk) if available at Persian grocery stores — their sharp flavour is more traditional than raisins in some Afghan households.
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Cook in a heavy-bottomed degchi or a thick-bottomed kadai. The braising stage can be done in a pressure cooker (3 whistles on medium) to reduce time by 30 minutes — just ensure there is enough broth remaining after pressure cooking for the rice stage. Basmati rice is a pantry staple. The dum technique is identical to biryani-making; seal the pot with atta dough for a tighter seal if needed. Raisins (kishmish) and almonds (badam) are available in every Indian grocery.
